topic: Someone receives flowers with no card attached
raw_output:
[story 1]

The box sat on the chipped kitchen table, an incongruous splash of vibrant color against the dull, grey backdrop of my apartment. Roses, impossibly perfect, their petals velvety and heavy with a scent that felt too luxurious for my current reality. I didn't touch the wrapping paper; my fingers just hovered above it, trembling slightly. There was no note, no whisper of who had taken the time to arrange this silent offering. My breath hitched in my throat, a shallow, ragged sound. I stared at the arrangement, my gaze darting around the empty room as if searching for an explanation, a lifeline. A sudden, hot prickle spread across my skin, and I gripped the edge of the table, knuckles whitening. This gesture felt enormous, a weight I simply couldn't bear to hold.

[story 2]

When the delivery man handed over the bouquet, the paper felt cool and crisp beneath my fingertips. They were lilies, stark white against the muted tones of my living space. I took the flowers with a stiffness that betrayed the tremor running through my hands. I didn't look up immediately, focusing instead on the way the light caught the dew-kissed edges of the petals. Every second felt stretched thin, taut as a wire about to snap. I retreated to the sofa, sinking into the cushions as if trying to melt into the upholstery. My chest felt tight, constricted, and a strange, hollow ache settled deep in my core. I traced the stem with my thumb, searching for any clue, any sign that this was meant for me, that this extravagance was real, not some cruel, misplaced kindness.

[story 3]

The flowers arrived late, tucked into the entryway where I’d been waiting, forgotten. They were sunflowers, blazing yellow, demanding attention. I found them sitting on the worn welcome mat, and a wave of nausea washed over me. I knelt down slowly, the movement stiff, and picked one up. The warmth of the petals against my cold palm was startling. I didn't bother examining the stems; I just needed to hold something tangible, something that wasn't the endless cycle of worry churning inside my head. My vision blurred momentarily, and I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to steady the frantic rhythm of my heart. If someone had done this, what did it mean? The silence of the apartment amplified the frantic drumming in my ears, making the sheer presence of the bright, cheerful blooms feel overwhelming, almost unbearable.
